quiz.title: Unit 2 Quiz 10, Biology I
quiz.subtitle: Ecology: Decomposition & Primary Productivity
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Decomposition Rates, GPP, NPP, Productivity Measurement

questions.question: What environment is favorable for the process of decomposition?
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) thrive in warm, moist environments with oxygen. Warm temperatures increase metabolic rates, and moisture is essential for microbial activity.

questions.question:
What environment is unfavorable for the process of decomposition?

i. low temperature
ii. high temperature
iii. low oxygen
iv. high oxygen
questions.explanation: Low temperature (i) slows decomposer metabolism, and low oxygen (iii) limits decomposer activity. High temperature and high oxygen actually favor decomposition.

questions.question: What is the total rate at which solar energy is captured and stored as organic matter by producers before any is used for their own respiration?
questions.explanation: Gross Primary Productivity (GPP) is the total rate of photosynthesis, capturing energy before any is used for respiration. NPP is what remains after respiration (GPP - R).

questions.question: In ecology, 'Productivity' is most accurately defined as the rate of:
options: Biomass production over a specific time period
questions.explanation: Productivity is the rate at which biomass is produced per unit area per unit time. It measures energy capture and storage by producers or consumers.

questions.question: Which of the following is a standard unit used to measure primary productivity?
questions.explanation: Primary productivity is typically measured in energy units per area per time, such as kilocalories per square meter per year (kcal/m²/yr) or grams of biomass per square meter per year.

questions.question: Primary productivity is typically divided into which two categories?
questions.explanation: Primary productivity is divided into Gross Primary Productivity (GPP — total energy captured) and Net Primary Productivity (NPP — energy remaining after respiration).

questions.question: Which of the following ecosystems typically exhibits the highest rate of primary productivity per square meter?
questions.explanation: Coral reefs have among the highest primary productivity rates due to symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae). Open ocean, deserts, and tundra have low productivity.

questions.question: When a cow eats grass and builds new muscle tissue, the rate at which this new consumer biomass is created is called:
questions.explanation: Secondary productivity is the rate at which consumers (heterotrophs) convert ingested organic matter into their own new biomass. Primary productivity refers to producers (plants/algae).

questions.question: A frog that eats an herbivorous insect is classified as a:
questions.explanation: A frog that eats an herbivorous insect is a secondary consumer. The herbivorous insect is the primary consumer (eating plants), so the frog that eats it occupies the next trophic level.

questions.question: Which formula correctly represents the relationship between Gross Primary Productivity (GPP), Respiration (R), and Net Primary Productivity (NPP)?
questions.explanation: NPP = GPP - R. Net Primary Productivity is what remains after producers use energy for their own respiration.

questions.question: What term describes the energy or biomass that is actually available for heterotrophs (consumers) to eat?
questions.explanation: Net Primary Productivity (NPP) is the energy remaining after producer respiration — this is the biomass available to consumers (herbivores) in an ecosystem.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You understand decomposition factors and productivity concepts clearly.
scoreMessages.70: Good job. Review the difference between GPP and NPP and decomposition conditions.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on what affects decomposition rates and productivity formulas.
scoreMessages.0: Please review decomposition ecology and primary productivity measurements.
